title: Round X-Base Table
summary: A round-top accent table on a splayed X-base of 2x2s, tied by two crossed stretchers top and bottom. The 5° bevels make the legs lean out — the fussy but rewarding part.
description:
This is a round side/accent table built on an X-base: four 2x2 legs that splay outward, braced by a crossed X-stretcher at the top and another at the bottom. It's a small, elegant piece, and the whole character comes from the legs leaning out rather than standing straight, which is set by a handful of 5° bevel cuts.

The X-stretchers are built like a lap-less half-lap: a long 2x2 run through the center with two shorter pieces screwed to each side, all beveled at 5° so the assembly tapers downward and the legs can lean. Build the top X first, mark its center carefully (13-7/8" from the outsides of the long board), and join it. Attach the legs so they lean outward, then build the bottom X the same way and tie the legs at the 6" mark from their ends.

The aprons under the top are the part the source builder found trickiest, and the key insight is counterintuitive: the aprons stay perpendicular to the ground, NOT parallel to the leaning legs. Once you accept that the apron doesn't follow the leg line, they go on cleanly — line up the inside corners and either nail (a brad nailer is very handy here) or predrill and screw them to the top X. The top is a store-bought 36" round or a plywood/MDF circle you cut yourself. Pocket screws or 3" screws throughout.

tools.note: Recommended for the X-joints; 3" screws at an angle also work.
For the repeated 5° bevel cuts that make the legs lean.
Only if you cut your own round top from sheet goods.

materials.name: Round tabletop 36 in, or 3/4 in MDF/plywood for a 36 in circle
Wood screws, 3 in, or pocket screws 2 in

cutList.note: One end cut at 5° off square, to long point.
Both ends cut at 5° off square, long point to long point.
One end cut at 5° off square, to long point.
Both ends cut at 5° off square, long point to long point.
Both ends cut at 45°, perpendicular to each other.

steps.title: Build the top X
steps.body: Cut all the boards as directed, and lay them out so the bevel cuts all taper downward. Mark the center of the long board (13-7/8" from the outsides), then screw the two short arms to the long board. Pocket screws are ideal; without a jig, drive screws at an angle to join the shorter boards to the long one.
materials: Wood screws, 3 in, or pocket screws 2 in

steps.title: Attach the legs
steps.body: Attach the four legs to the top X so that each leg leans outward. The 5° bevels on the X are what let the legs splay — check that all four lean the same amount so the base is symmetric.
materials: Wood screws, 3 in, or pocket screws 2 in

steps.title: Build and attach the bottom X
steps.body: Build the bottom X exactly as you built the top X. Mark all four legs 6" from their ends, then attach the bottom X to the legs at those marks. The lower stretcher braces the splayed legs and keeps the base rigid.
materials: Wood screws, 3 in, or pocket screws 2 in

steps.title: Add the aprons
steps.body: This is the part that trips people up. Line up the inside corners of the aprons and attach them to the legs and top X — glue and nail (a brad nailer helps a lot here) or predrill and screw them to the top X. The key: the aprons stay perpendicular to the ground, they do NOT follow the line of the leaning legs. Accept that and they go on cleanly.
materials: Wood screws, 3 in, or pocket screws 2 in

steps.title: Attach the top and finish
steps.body: Center the round top on the base and attach it — through the aprons or the top X from underneath. If you cut your own top from MDF or plywood, cut the 36" circle with a jigsaw and sand the edge smooth first. Sand the base and finish.
materials: Round tabletop 36 in, or 3/4 in MDF/plywood for a 36 in circle

images.alt: Round-top accent table on a splayed X-shaped base
